questions.question: Which description best matches canonical TDD in this course?

options.A: Write all tests for the feature, then implement the system in one pass
options.B: Write one failing behavior check, make it pass with a small change, then refactor as needed
options.C: Implement the design first, then add enough tests to reach coverage goals
options.D: Focus on mocks first so refactoring is rarely necessary

questions.explanation: The course defines canonical TDD as a small-step behavior-design loop rather than as batch test writing or post-hoc verification.

questions.question: What is the strongest sign that the next TDD step is too large?

options.A: The test requires several future abstractions to be imagined before it can be written
options.B: The function name is still short
options.C: The code under test has not imported pytest yet
options.D: The last test passed on the first run

questions.explanation: When the next test forces you to invent multiple future abstractions, the step is probably too large to give good design feedback.

questions.question: After a test goes green, when should you refactor immediately?

options.A: Always, because every green step requires visible restructuring
options.B: Only when the code now makes the next likely behavior harder to express or extend cleanly
options.C: Never, because refactoring breaks TDD flow
options.D: Only at the end of the whole feature

questions.explanation: The course resolves the refactoring tension by asking whether the latest change increases design pressure for the next step.

questions.question: Explain why the course treats TDD as a design discipline rather than just a testing habit.

required_points: Tests expose the next needed behavior before implementation grows
The loop shapes interface and structure incrementally
Refactoring keeps the design easy to extend instead of merely checking finished code
answer_key.full_credit: Explains how behavior-first tests and refactoring guide design rather than only verify code after the fact
answer_key.partial_credit: Mentions test-first or refactoring but not how they shape design

questions.prompt: A teammate writes most of a feature, then backfills tests and calls the workflow TDD. Use Module 1 to explain what they are missing and how you would restructure the work into smaller red-green-refactor cycles.
questions.guidance: Strong answers explain behavior-first sequencing, small-step control, and refactoring triggered by design pressure rather than ritual.

rubric.A: Clearly distinguishes backfilled testing from TDD and proposes a credible small-step redesign workflow
rubric.B: Identifies some TDD concepts but leaves the redesign plan vague
rubric.C: Mostly restates that tests should come first without explaining the design implications
